title: Psoriasis and Sleep: Why Nights Can Be Hard

title.value: Psoriasis and Sleep: Why Nights Can Be Hard

summary.value: If psoriasis is disrupting your sleep, you're far from alone. Understanding the link between skin inflammation and sleep is the first step toward better nights.

heroImage.altText: Sleep and night sky illustration with crescent moon and stars on indigo-blue gradient

body.value:
Why psoriasis and sleep don't always get along

If you find yourself lying awake at night with your skin itching and your mind restless, you are not unusual. Research suggests that between 40 and 80% of people with psoriasis experience significant sleep disruption — making it one of the most commonly reported effects of the condition on daily life.

Nocturnal itch tends to intensify at night for two main reasons: the natural drop in body temperature triggers an increase in skin blood flow heightening itch signals; and reduced distraction in a quiet, dark room means the brain processes itch more intensely.

The sleep-inflammation cycle

Poor sleep elevates cortisol (your stress hormone), and elevated cortisol can increase systemic inflammation — which in turn can worsen your psoriasis and intensify itch the following night. Even small, consistent improvements to sleep quality can have a meaningful knock-on effect on how you feel during the day.

Practical steps for better sleep

  • Keep your bedroom cool — a room temperature of around 16–18°C supports deeper sleep and reduces night-time itch triggers.
  • Choose breathable bedding — 100% cotton or bamboo sheets are gentle on skin and don't trap heat.
  • Apply moisturiser before bed — a thick, fragrance-free emollient can reduce overnight skin drying.
  • Shower in cool or lukewarm water — a cool shower before bed lowers body temperature and can calm itch.
  • Limit alcohol and caffeine — both disturb sleep quality and alcohol is a recognised psoriasis trigger.
  • Maintain a consistent sleep schedule — going to bed and waking at the same time strengthens your circadian rhythm.
  • Reduce screen time before bed — blue light delays melatonin release.

When to mention it to your care team

If your sleep is significantly disrupted several nights a week, it's worth raising with your dermatologist or GP. Cognitive Behavioural Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) has a strong evidence base and is increasingly available.

This content is for educational purposes only and is not a substitute for personalised advice from your healthcare team.
